items.title: Understanding the Etiology of Palpitations
items.content: Palpitations can be attributed to a variety of causes, with cardiac issues being the most common (43%), followed by psychiatric causes (31%). It is crucial for clinicians to differentiate between these etiologies as they guide management and treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Cardiac causes are responsible for 43% of palpitations cases.
Psychiatric causes account for 31% of palpitations cases.
Miscellaneous causes make up 10%, with unknown causes at 16%.

items.title: Characteristics and Diagnostic Approach to Palpitations
items.content: The sensation of palpitations can vary in duration, regularity, and associated symptoms. Patients with psychiatric causes often report longer durations (>15 min) and additional symptoms compared to other etiologies. Clinicians should assess the rhythm and pulse during palpitations for diagnostic clarity.
keyPoints: Psychiatric causes often involve palpitations lasting >15 minutes with additional symptoms.
Assessing the rhythm by having patients 'tap out' or taking their pulse can aid diagnosis.
Most arrhythmias are not associated with palpitations, but when they are, these assessments are useful.

items.title: Diagnosing Arrhythmias in Patients with Palpitations
items.content: To diagnose arrhythmias causing palpitations, use a combination of physical examination and diagnostic tests. Measurement of vital signs, jugular venous pressure, pulse, and chest auscultation are crucial. A resting ECG can document the arrhythmia, while exercise ECG is useful if exertion induces symptoms. For infrequent arrhythmias, consider Holter monitoring, telephonic monitoring, loop recordings (external or implantable), and mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry.
keyPoints: Holter monitoring has limited clinical utility.
Implantable loop recorders and mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry are safe and possibly more cost-effective for infrequent palpitations.
Use a diary or electronic marker to time palpitations for appropriate study interpretation.

items.title: Management of Palpitations Based on Underlying Causes
items.content: Most patients with palpitations do not have serious arrhythmias or structural heart disease. Benign atrial or ventricular premature contractions can be managed with beta-blockers if bothersome. Address palpitations from alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs through abstention and consider alternative therapies for those caused by pharmacologic agents. Psychiatric causes may benefit from cognitive therapy or pharmacotherapy.
keyPoints: Most palpitations are not due to serious arrhythmias or structural heart disease.
Beta-blockers can manage occasional benign premature contractions.
Reassure patients that palpitations generally do not affect overall prognosis once serious causes are excluded.

items.question: Which cardiac cause is associated with palpitations due to the sensation of a 'post-extrasystolic beat'?
items.explanation: Premature atrial or ventricular contractions cause a post-extrasystolic beat, which is sensed by the patient due to increased contraction strength.

items.question: What percentage of palpitation cases are attributed to psychiatric causes according to one large series?
items.explanation: Psychiatric causes account for 31% of palpitations in the cited large series.

items.question: Which condition is commonly associated with positional palpitations?
items.explanation: Positional palpitations often reflect structural processes such as atrial myxoma.

items.question: Which method is suggested to be more cost-effective in assessing patients with infrequent recurrent unexplained palpitations?
items.explanation: The implantable loop recorder and mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry are suggested to be safe and possibly more cost-effective.

items.question: What is essential for the appropriate interpretation of studies involving continuous electrocardiographic monitoring?
options: Use of a diary or electronic marker
items.explanation: The use of a diary or an electronic marker to indicate the timing of palpitations sensed by the patient is essential.

items.question: Which management strategy is recommended for benign atrial or ventricular premature contractions causing palpitations?
items.explanation: Occasional benign atrial or ventricular premature contractions can often be managed with beta-blocker therapy.

items.statement: Most arrhythmias are associated with palpitations.
items.explanation: Most arrhythmias are not associated with palpitations, though some can be.

items.statement: Patients with psychiatric causes of palpitations often report a duration of the sensation longer than 15 minutes.
items.explanation: Psychiatric causes for palpitations are more commonly reported to last longer than other causes.

items.statement: Most patients with palpitations have serious arrhythmias or underlying structural heart disease.
items.explanation: Most patients with palpitations do not have serious arrhythmias or underlying structural heart disease.

items.statement: Psychiatric causes of palpitations may benefit from cognitive therapy or pharmacotherapy.
items.explanation: Psychiatric causes of palpitations can indeed benefit from cognitive therapy or pharmacotherapy.
